Monthly P&L Comparison
Austin Clarkson & Team

Current Model (Lower.com) vs. Rapid Mortgage P&L Branch

Confidential — For Discussion Purposes Only

1 Production Baseline (Monthly)

All figures are derived from Austin's reported annual production of $110.64M across 356 units, converted to a monthly run rate.

$9.22M
Monthly Volume
29.7
Monthly Units
$310K
Avg Loan Size
Team MemberMonthly VolumeMonthly Units
Austin Clarkson (Lead)$4.61M14.8
Partner LO #1$2.77M8.9
Partner LO #2$1.84M5.9
Total Team$9.22M29.7

2 Key Assumptions

This analysis uses industry-standard assumptions for both retail and broker compensation models. Where actual figures differ, the framework holds — only the inputs change.

AssumptionLower.com (Retail)Rapid (Broker P&L)
Gross Revenue per Loan (bps on volume)275 bps225 bps
Retail shops charge higher lender fees; broker model passes savings to borrower but operates on leaner margin.
LO Comp Split (% of gross revenue)35%N/A (P&L model)
At retail shops, LOs typically receive 80-110 bps (roughly 30-40% of gross margin). At Rapid, Austin runs the branch P&L and keeps net profit.
Branch P&L Net Share to AustinN/A70%
After all branch expenses, Austin's team keeps 70% of net profit. Rapid retains 30% for corporate overhead, compliance, and warehouse line access.
Processing Cost per File$650$0 (provided by Rapid)
Lower charges processing per file. Rapid provides a dedicated processor as part of the branch support package.
LO Comp to Partner LOs (paid by Austin)Paid by Lower100 bps each
In the P&L model, Austin pays his own team. Industry standard for partner LOs under a branch lead is 80-120 bps.
Office / Desk Fees$750/mo per LO$0 (provided by Rapid)
Tech / Systems$350/mo per LO$0 (provided by Rapid)
E&O Insurance$200/mo (Austin's share)$150/mo (lower group rate)
Marketing$500/mo (Austin funds own)$800/mo (Austin funds own)
Higher marketing budget at Rapid reflects loss of national brand recognition. Austin would invest more in local branding, especially in Year 1.
Transparency Note: These assumptions are meant to be discussed and adjusted with real numbers. If Austin's actual comp at Lower is higher or lower than 35% of gross, the model recalibrates accordingly. The goal is an honest framework, not a sales pitch.

3 Current Monthly P&L at Lower.com

Under the retail model, Austin earns a commission split on his personal production. His partner LOs are compensated separately by the company. Austin does not control margins, pricing, or team economics.

Line ItemMonthlyNotes
Revenue
Austin's Personal Volume $4,610,000 $55.32M / 12
Gross Revenue at 275 bps $126,775 Company-level revenue on Austin's loans
Austin's Compensation
Commission at 35% of Gross (approx. 96 bps) $44,372 Standard retail LO split
Austin's Direct Expenses
Processing ($650 x 14.8 files) ($9,620) Per-file charge on Austin's production
Desk Fee ($750) Monthly seat cost
Tech / Systems ($350) CRM, LOS access, tools
E&O Insurance ($200) Pro-rated share
Marketing (self-funded) ($500) Digital, print, local sponsorships
Total Expenses ($11,420)
Net to Austin (Monthly) $32,930 Take-home before taxes

Key limitation: Austin has no visibility into the remaining $82,403/mo in gross revenue that Lower retains from his personal production alone. He also has no economic participation in the $46.1M/yr his partner LOs produce. He built the team — but the company captures all the upside from their volume.

4 Proposed Monthly P&L at Rapid Mortgage

Under the P&L branch model, Austin controls the full economics of his branch. Revenue from the entire team flows through the branch P&L. After paying operating expenses and partner LO comp, Austin keeps 70% of net profit.

Line ItemMonthlyNotes
Branch Gross Revenue
Total Team Volume $9,220,000 All 3 producers combined
Gross Revenue at 225 bps $207,450 Broker margin (lower than retail)
Branch Operating Expenses
Partner LO #1 Comp (100 bps on $2.77M) ($27,700) Competitive LO pay
Partner LO #2 Comp (100 bps on $1.84M) ($18,400) Competitive LO pay
Processing Staff $0 Provided by Rapid corporate
Office Space $0 Provided by Rapid corporate
Tech / Systems $0 Provided by Rapid corporate
E&O Insurance ($150) Group rate through Rapid
Marketing (Austin-funded) ($800) Higher spend to build local brand
Misc Branch Expenses ($500) Supplies, travel, client events
Total Branch Expenses ($47,550)
Branch Net Profit
Net Profit Before Split $159,900 Gross revenue minus all expenses
Profit Split
Austin's Share (70%) $111,930 P&L branch partner share
Rapid Corporate (30%) $47,970 License, compliance, warehouse lines, support
Net to Austin (Monthly) $111,930 Take-home before taxes

What changes: Austin is no longer a commissioned employee. He operates a business unit. He pays his own people, controls his own margins, and captures profit from the entire team's production — not just his own book.

5 Monthly Delta

The net difference in Austin's monthly take-home, holding production constant at current levels.

$32,930
Lower.com Net/Mo
$111,930
Rapid Net/Mo
+$78,978
Monthly Uplift
MetricLower.comRapid Mortgage
Annual Net to Austin $395,155 $1,343,160
Annual Uplift +$948,005 / year
Effective Bps on Total Team Volume 36 bps (on personal only) 121 bps (on total team)
Economic Participation in Team Volume Austin's book only (50%) Entire team (100%)
Equity Upside None Ownership stake in Rapid
Margin Control Set by corporate Austin controls pricing

6 Honest Tradeoffs

No move is without tradeoffs. Here is what Austin would gain and what he would give up.

Where Rapid Wins

  • Dramatically higher take-home: The P&L model pays Austin as a business owner, not an employee. At his volume, the math is not close.
  • Team economics: Austin built his team. At Rapid, he participates in their production. At Lower, he does not.
  • Equity: Real ownership in a growing company. No retail lender offers this to an LO.
  • Lower borrower costs: Broker model means lower lender fees, which makes Austin more competitive on rate and closing costs.
  • Autonomy: No corporate pricing desk overrides. No bureaucracy on underwriting exceptions. Direct line to ownership.
  • Processing included: Dedicated processor provided by Rapid eliminates a $9,600+/mo expense.

Where Lower Wins (or Where Risk Exists)

  • Brand recognition: Lower.com is a known national brand. Rapid Mortgage does not have the same consumer awareness. Austin would need to invest more in personal branding and local marketing, especially in Year 1.
  • Operational responsibility: The P&L model means Austin owns expenses. If production dips, the fixed costs do not. At Lower, comp just scales down — there is no negative P&L risk.
  • Transition disruption: Pipeline in progress, realtor relationships, referral partners — any move creates 30-60 days of transition friction. Some loans in process may not transfer cleanly.
  • Benefits: If Lower provides health insurance, 401(k) match, or other employment benefits, these have real value (typically $15K-25K/yr) that must be factored in. Rapid's compensation uplift covers this significantly, but it is a real consideration.
  • Marketing support: Large retail shops often have in-house marketing teams, branded events, and lead generation programs. At Rapid, Austin funds his own marketing — the upside is full control, the downside is full responsibility.
Bottom line: The financial case is strong. The tradeoffs are real but manageable. The biggest risk factor is transition friction, which is temporary. The biggest upside — ownership economics and team participation — is permanent.

7 Sensitivity Analysis

What happens if production changes or assumptions shift? The model remains favorable across a wide range of scenarios.

ScenarioLower Net/MoRapid Net/MoDelta
Current production (baseline) $32,930 $111,930 +$78,978
Production drops 20% $25,984 $89,341 +$63,357
Production drops 30% $22,511 $78,046 +$55,536
Austin's Lower comp is actually 40% (not 35%) $39,268 $111,930 +$72,662
Rapid split is 60/40 (not 70/30) $32,930 $95,940 +$63,010
Partner LO comp at 120 bps (not 100) $32,930 $105,476 +$72,546
Worst case (all negatives combined) $26,948 $63,025 +$36,077

Even in the worst-case scenario — where Austin's actual Lower comp is higher than estimated, production drops 30%, the Rapid split is less favorable, and partner LO comp is higher — Austin still nets an additional $36K+/month at Rapid. The structural advantage of the P&L model is that significant at this volume level.

8 The Bottom Line

+$948K
Annual Uplift (Baseline)
+$433K
Annual Uplift (Worst Case)
3.4x
Income Multiple (Baseline)

Austin's team is producing at an elite level. The question is not whether they can produce — it is whether the current structure captures the full value of what they have built.

At Lower, Austin earns a commission on his personal book. At Rapid, he operates a business with full economic participation in the team he built, an equity stake in the parent company, and the autonomy to run his branch his way.

These numbers are meant to start a conversation, not end one. Every assumption is adjustable. The framework is what matters: the P&L model pays more because it structurally gives Austin more.

Rapid Mortgage
Mark Jaynes, Co-Owner
rapidmortgage.com
Confidential. All figures are estimates based on industry standard assumptions and reported production data. Actual results will vary based on negotiated terms, market conditions, and production levels. Not a binding offer.
